names.transliteration: Taqī al-Dīn Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad ibn ʿAbd al-Ḥalīm ibn Taymiyya al-Ḥarrānī

summary: Ibn Taymiyya (1263-1328 CE) was a Hanbali jurist, traditionist, and theologian active in Damascus. Working from a textualist and traditionist commitment, he critiqued the methods of kalam and falsafa and developed an influential account of tawhid that gives primacy to a distinction between lordship (rububiyya) and devotion (uluhiyya); the fixed threefold division of tawhid associated with his name in later usage is largely a development of the later Athari/Salafi reception.
description: Ibn Taymiyya was born in Harran, in the Jazira, and his family relocated to Damascus when he was a child, fleeing the Mongol advance; the nisba al-Harrani preserves the birthplace. He was trained in the Hanbali madhhab and taught in Damascus, and much of his career was marked by scholarly controversy and periods of imprisonment arising from his theological and legal positions. Against the theologians of kalam and the philosophers (falasifa), he argued that authentic reason does not contradict authentic revelation, a thesis developed at length in his major work Dar' Ta'arud al-'Aql wa-l-Naql ('The Averting of the Conflict between Reason and Revelation'); on this view, where the two appear to conflict, the fault lies in a defective rational premise or an unsound transmission rather than in revelation. He elaborated at length the substance of a distinction between tawhid al-rububiyya (the oneness of God as sole lord and creator) and tawhid al-uluhiyya (the directing of worship to God alone), charging the Ash'ari theologians with reducing tawhid largely to lordship while neglecting devotion; on Jon Hoover's reading his own framing gives primacy to a twofold division, tied to a linguistic distinction between informative (khabar) and performative (insha') speech. The fixed tripartite scheme that adds tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat (the affirmation of the divine names and attributes as revealed) as a third named category, and that is commonly attributed to him today, is largely a feature of the later Athari/Salafi reception: the expression tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat does not appear as a settled formula in his own writings, and the third category is often credited to the eighteenth-century reformer Ibn Abd al-Wahhab. On the divine attributes he affirmed the attested names and attributes without figurative reinterpretation (ta'wil), a stance he presented as fidelity to the way of the early community. His works became a principal reference point for the later Athari (traditionist) current and for the Hanbali school, and their interpretation and reception remain the subject of active scholarly debate.
editorial_notes: Birth and death years (661/1263 - 728/1328) are as commonly reported and are given as approximate in the lifespan note. Birthplace is Harran (not Damascus); Damascus was where he was raised, taught, and died, and the entry states this explicitly to avoid the common conflation flagged during research. Attribution of the tawhid scheme (2026-07-19, peer review fix): the entry previously overstated Ibn Taymiyya's authorship of the fixed tripartite division (rububiyya / uluhiyya / al-asma' wa-l-sifat). Per Jon Hoover, "The Tawḥīds of Ibn Taymiyya" (2025), Ibn Taymiyya elaborated the substance of the rububiyya/uluhiyya distinction and gives theological primacy to a twofold division tied to a linguistic distinction between informative (khabar) and performative (insha') speech; the expression tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat does not occur as a settled formula in his own writings, and the fixed threefold scheme common among Salafis today is largely a feature of the later Athari/Salafi reception, with the third category often credited (following Lauziere) to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab. The prose in the summary, description, and the 'articulated tawhid' relation has been softened accordingly to attribute the fixed tripartite scheme to the later reception rather than to Ibn Taymiyya's own fixed formula, per neutrality principle #1 (describe, do not adjudicate). This one point (Hoover's argument) was read directly from the chapter preview and is rated high; the rest of the entry remains medium. His critique of the Ash'aris is presented as his attributed position, not as an adjudication of the dispute. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) modern Western academic scholarship (Hoover, Brill 2007, and Hoover's 2025 "Tawhids" chapter), (b) Ibn Taymiyya's own primary work Dar' Ta'arud al-'Aql wa-l-Naql in the standard Rashad Salim critical edition, and (c) a contemporary Muslim scholar (El-Tobgui, Brill 2020). The El-Tobgui "Open access (OAPEN)" claim was verified (2026-07-19): the book is open access and freely available via OAPEN (library.oapen.org) and listed in the DOAB; the claim is retained. Confidence is medium except where noted: the other sources were verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher and review records, but their relevant passages were not read line-by-line.

lifespan.note: 661-728 AH / 1263-1328 CE; born in Harran, active and died in Damascus. Years as commonly reported.

relations.note: Trained and taught within the Hanbali madhhab.
A principal reference point and key figure for the later Athari (traditionist) current.

Elaborated the substance of a distinction between rububiyya and uluhiyya, giving primacy (per Hoover) to a twofold division; the fixed tripartite scheme adding al-asma' wa-l-sifat is largely a later Athari/Salafi development, the third category often credited to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab.
Affirmed the divine names and attributes as revealed, without figurative reinterpretation.
Engaged the question of transcendence, affirming the attributes while denying resemblance to creation.
Critiqued the methods of speculative theology; the critique is attributed to him, not adjudicated.
Critiqued the philosophers on the relation of reason and revelation in Dar' Ta'arud al-'Aql wa-l-Naql.

sources.citation: Hoover, Jon. Ibn Taymiyya's Theodicy of Perpetual Optimism. Islamic Philosophy, Theology and Science: Texts and Studies, vol. 73. Leiden and Boston: Brill, 2007.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; text not reproduced.

sources.citation: Hoover, Jon. "The Tawḥīds of Ibn Taymiyya." In Islamic Perspectives on God and (Other) Monotheism(s), edited by Wahid M. Amin, Aaron W. Hughes, and Sajjad H. Rizvi, 89-110. AMI Press, 2025. Argues that Ibn Taymiyya gives primacy to a twofold tawhid (rububiyya/uluhiyya) tied to the khabar/insha' speech distinction, that the expression tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat does not occur as a settled formula in his writings, and that the fixed threefold division common among later Salafis is largely a later development, with the third category credited by Lauziere to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab.
sources.license: Publisher chapter preview; cited for reference.

sources.citation: Ibn Taymiyya, Taqi al-Din. Dar' Ta'arud al-'Aql wa-l-Naql (aw Muwafaqat Sahih al-Manqul li-Sarih al-Ma'qul). Edited by Muhammad Rashad Salim, 11 vols. Riyadh: Jami'at al-Imam Muhammad ibn Sa'ud al-Islamiyya, 1399-1403 AH / 1979-1983. Primary source: Ibn Taymiyya's own work arguing that authentic reason and authentic revelation do not conflict.
sources.license: Classical text; cited for reference (relevant passages not read line-by-line).

sources.citation: El-Tobgui, Carl Sharif. Ibn Taymiyya on Reason and Revelation: A Study of Dar' Ta'arud al-'Aql wa-l-Naql. Islamic Philosophy, Theology and Science: Texts and Studies, vol. 111. Leiden and Boston: Brill, 2020.
